label: MySQL
description: Connect to a MySQL (or MariaDB/Percona) database to read and write data using SQL.

form.title: Connect MySQL
form.subtitle: Provide your MySQL host, credentials, and optional SSL settings to connect.

methods.label: Host & Password
methods.description: Enter your MySQL host, port, database name, username, and password. Supports SSL for encrypted connections. Works with any MySQL-compatible server.
methods.how_to:
## Connect with Host & Password

This is the standard way to connect Anton to any MySQL-compatible database (MySQL, MariaDB, Percona, PlanetScale, etc.).

### Steps

1. Locate your database connection details — these are typically found in your hosting dashboard, cloud provider console, or from your database administrator.
2. Enter the **Host** — this is the hostname or IP address of your MySQL server (e.g. `db.example.com` or `127.0.0.1`).
3. Enter the **Port** — MySQL defaults to `3306`. Change this only if your server uses a non-standard port.
4. Enter the **Database** name — the specific database (schema) Anton should connect to.
5. Enter the **Username** and **Password** for a MySQL user that has the permissions you need.
6. Toggle **Use SSL** on if your server requires or supports encrypted connections (recommended for remote servers).
7. If using SSL with a custom CA certificate (common with managed cloud databases like AWS RDS or Google Cloud SQL), paste the CA certificate contents into **SSL CA Certificate**.

### Notes

- Make sure the MySQL user has been granted access from the host Anton runs on. If connecting remotely, run: `GRANT ALL ON yourdb.* TO 'user'@'%' IDENTIFIED BY 'password';` and ensure the firewall/security group allows inbound TCP on port 3306.
- For cloud-managed databases (AWS RDS, Google Cloud SQL, Azure Database for MySQL), SSL is usually required — enable the toggle and paste the provider's CA cert.
- Anton stores your password encrypted locally and never transmits it to Anton's servers.

fields.name: host
fields.label: Host
fields.description: Hostname or IP address of your MySQL server.

fields.name: database
fields.label: Database
fields.description: The name of the specific database (schema) to connect to.

fields.name: username
fields.label: Username
fields.description: MySQL user with the appropriate permissions for this database.

fields.name: password
fields.label: Password
fields.description: Password for the MySQL user. Stored encrypted locally.

fields.name: use_ssl
fields.label: Use SSL
fields.description: Enable SSL/TLS encryption for the connection. Recommended for remote or cloud-hosted databases.

fields.name: ssl_ca_cert
fields.label: SSL CA Certificate
fields.description: Optional. Paste the PEM-encoded CA certificate to verify the server's identity. Required by some managed cloud databases (e.g. AWS RDS, Google Cloud SQL).

methods.label: Connection String (DSN)
methods.description: Paste a full MySQL connection string (DSN). Useful if your tooling or hosting provider already gives you one.
methods.how_to:
## Connect with a MySQL Connection String (DSN)

If your hosting provider or framework already provides a full MySQL DSN, paste it here instead of filling in individual fields.

### MySQL DSN format

```
mysql://username:password@host:port/database
```

### Steps

1. Obtain your DSN from your hosting dashboard, cloud console (e.g. PlanetScale, Railway, Heroku, Render), or application config file.
2. Paste the full DSN into the **Connection String** field below.
3. If SSL is required, append `?ssl=true` or `?sslmode=require` to the DSN, or add your CA cert in the **SSL CA Certificate** field.

### Notes

- The DSN contains your password in plain text — treat it as a secret and avoid sharing it.
- If your password contains special characters (e.g. `@`, `/`, `#`), ensure they are URL-encoded in the DSN.
- PlanetScale and similar serverless MySQL providers may supply DSNs directly from their dashboards under **Connect → Connect with → Generic**.

fields.name: connection_string
fields.label: Connection String
fields.description: Full MySQL DSN. Includes host, port, credentials, and database name. Treated as a secret.

fields.name: ssl_ca_cert
fields.label: SSL CA Certificate
fields.description: Optional. Paste the PEM-encoded CA certificate if your server requires SSL certificate verification.
